title: Worship
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
outline: Introduction: A standalone lesson on worship before the discipleship series
Context: the conclusion of 'What is the Mission of the Church'
The mission of the church as making disciples
Elements and purpose of corporate worship
The Lord's Day as the primary occasion for worship
Conclusion: Worship as foundational to the church's mission
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches a standalone AM lesson on worship in anticipation of beginning the discipleship series the following Lord's Day. Having concluded the 'Mission of the Church' series — which establishes that the church's mission is to make disciples — he presents worship as an essential and foundational practice of the Christian life.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why This Topic

Lord willing, next Lord's Day we begin a long series on discipleship, working through the book *What Is the Mission of the Church?* — the conclusion of which is that the mission of the church is to make disciples of all nations. That book does an excellent job explaining *why* that is the mission, but it does not address *how* to accomplish it. So we will piggyback on that and talk about discipleship in terms of parenting, marriage, being part of a local church, neighborhood, family, and more.

But today I want a standalone lesson on worship — and specifically, why do we do what we do here? Did we just pull our practices out of a bag with little papers, or is there some other basis for what we do? John Piper wrote a book called *Let the Nations Be Glad*, and he opens it with this statement: "Missions exists because worship doesn't." The reason we go to all the nations to proclaim the gospel is because they are not worshiping Jesus. The history of Scripture is the history of worship — from perfect worship before the Fall, to no worship for a time, to imperfect worship (what we do here), to perfect worship in the eternal state. Worship is the only activity we know for certain we will be doing forever. That makes it a supremely important subject.

## Two Definitions: Regulative and Normative Principles

When serious, thoughtful, Bible-believing Christians think about corporate worship, they generally fall into one of two categories. Let me give you the definitions.

**The Regulative Principle** says that everything we do in corporate worship must be clearly warranted by the Scriptures. Clear warrant takes the form of either an explicit biblical command or a good and necessary implication of a biblical text. In other words: the Bible either explicitly or implicitly requires it. That is what we may do.

**The Normative Principle** says that as long as a practice is not biblically *forbidden*, a church is free to use it in structuring its corporate worship life. In other words: whatever the Bible does not prohibit is permitted.

Do you see the difference? One says: only what the Bible commands — explicitly or implicitly. The other says: whatever the Bible does not forbid.

Which one do we follow here? The regulative principle — not just personally, but also constitutionally. It is in our confessional standards, the Westminster Standards, and we believe it is what the Bible teaches concerning the corporate worship of the church.

## Why the Regulative Principle?

**First, because the Bible says so.** That is really the bottom line. In Deuteronomy 12:32: "Whatever I command you, be careful to observe it. You shall not add to it, nor take away from it" — and the context is worship. Interestingly, in that chapter God says, "Don't worship false gods" — and we all get that. But he also says: "Don't worship the true God falsely." Sincerity and a desire to please him is not the ultimate criterion for whether something we do in corporate worship is right or wrong.

**Second, because conscience can only be bound by Scripture.** When we are leading people in worship, we are binding their consciences in each part of the service. And conscience can only be legitimately bound by the Word of God. Notice that when we ask you to stand for the reading of Scripture, we say "if you are able" — because we cannot say from Scripture that if you do not stand, you are sinning. So we say it is a fitting way to show respect, but we cannot require it. Singing, on the other hand: if you regularly choose not to sing in corporate worship, you are sinning. The Bible commands congregational singing. So we do say you *must* sing. When we say "must," we need to be able to prove it from Scripture.

**Third, because the Bible is full of examples of God caring about how his people worship him corporately.** Not just *that* they worship, but *how*. Have you ever read the description of the ark? The dimensions of the tabernacle? God gave us everything down to the angles and the materials. God is a precise God, a God of details. He did not leave it to the sinful imaginations of his people to figure out how he should be worshiped.

## Worship Is the Purpose of Redemption

Worship is not merely one thing the redeemed people of God do — it is the *purpose* of their redemption. Look at the Exodus narrative. From Exodus 3 through 10, every time Moses and Aaron approached Pharaoh, their message was: "Let my people go — so that they may **worship me**."

> *"I will certainly be with you, and this shall be a sign to you that I have sent you: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you shall serve [worship] God on this mountain."* (Exodus 3:12)

> *"Please let us go three days' journey into the wilderness that we may sacrifice to the Lord our God."* (Exodus 3:18)

> *"Thus says the Lord God of Israel: 'Let my people go, that they may hold a feast to me in the wilderness.'"* (Exodus 5:1)

The pattern continues through chapters 7, 8, 9, and 10 — every plea to Pharaoh is "free us that we might worship God." And if you read through Exodus and Deuteronomy, you will see that God not only picked the place and the time of corporate worship — he specified exactly how it was to be done. He did not say: "Come before me in sincerity of heart and whatever you do will be fine." He said: "Come in sincerity of heart — and do it in this place, at this time, in this way."

## God Cares About *How* We Worship in the Old Testament

The second commandment is instructive here. Look at Exodus 20:4–6:

> *"You shall not make for yourself a carved image — any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth; you shall not bow down to them nor serve them."* (Exodus 20:4–5)

Notice what it does *not* say. It does not say "do not worship other gods through images." That is the first commandment. The second commandment is about not worshiping *the true God* through images. In making the golden calf, Israel was not trying to worship a different god. Aaron said at the end: "Tomorrow is a feast to the LORD" — using the covenant name *Yahweh*. They were trying to worship the God who had delivered them from Egypt through the calf. And God's reaction was to threaten to destroy them all.

> *"Now therefore, let me alone, that my wrath may burn hot against them and I may consume them."* (Exodus 32:10)

When they tried to worship the true God falsely, God was ready to destroy them. That is how seriously he takes his worship.

## God Cares About *How* We Worship in the New Testament

Some might say: "That is the Old Testament." But the God of the New Testament is the same God. Look at John 4:19–24. Jesus tells the Samaritan woman that her worship was inadequate because it was based on a view of God informed only by a modified version of the Pentateuch — the Samaritans did not accept the rest of the Old Testament and had substituted Mount Gerizim for Jerusalem. Jesus says:

> *"You worship what you do not know; we know what we worship, for salvation is of the Jews... But the hour is coming, and now is, when the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth."* (John 4:22–23)

The Samaritans' sincerity was commendable. But sincerity is not sufficient for proper worship. Proper worship is a *response to whom God has revealed himself to be*. And if worship is a response to revelation, then it must accord with that revelation. God tells us who he is in the Bible. Therefore worship must be regulated by the Bible.

> *"Sanctify them by your truth. Your word is truth."* (John 17:17)

And in 1 Corinthians 14, Paul's entire chapter on corporate worship is structured around the principle that everything must be done for edification and according to the Word of God. In verse 33: "God is not the author of confusion but of peace, as in all the churches of the saints." He bases the whole structure of the worship service on the *character of God as revealed in Scripture*. Even the extraordinary apostolic gifts, while they were still operative, were regulated by revelation.

## How Does This Shape Our Sunday Worship?

Jesus is building his church by the power of his own Word (Matthew 16:18; Romans 10:17). He regulates the church's worship by that same Word. So how do we structure our worship to reflect this?

We do five things: **We read, we preach, we pray, we sing, and we see** the Word of God.

**We read the Bible.** In 1 Timothy 4:13, Paul tells Timothy: "Give attention to reading, to exhortation, to doctrine." If a preacher says "we don't have time to read the text today," that is a problem — the text is the only inspired thing that will be said all morning. Publicly reading Scripture is an element of worship, not merely preliminary material. God dispenses grace to his people through the public reading of his Word. "Blessed is he who reads and those who hear the words of this prophecy" (Revelation 1:3).

**We preach the Bible.** "Preach the word; be ready in season and out of season" (2 Timothy 4:2). In the sixteenth century, as Protestantism spread across Europe, church architecture changed. The communion table, previously fixed at the center of the room as the location of the Mass, became a movable table — and what was fixed in the center was the pulpit. Because the preaching of the Word is central to the worship of God. The hearing of the Word, like the preaching of it, is an active, worshipful act.

**We pray the Bible.** Christian prayer, at its best, is God's Word prayed back to him. In our corporate prayers we speak to God of his own attributes and promises as revealed in Scripture.

**We sing the Bible.** The whole congregation is commanded to sing to one another as a sign of being filled with the Spirit (Ephesians 5:18–19; Colossians 3:16). We sing songs filled with the Word — old hymns and new songs alike, all judged by their fidelity to Scripture.

**We see the Bible — in the sacraments.** Baptism and the Lord's Supper are the drama God has given to his church. In the Lord's Supper we can smell, touch, see, taste, and hear the bread and the cup — all our senses engaging the gospel of Jesus Christ. The water of baptism, the bread and the cup, are the dramatic elements God has prescribed. We do not need to add human drama to worship. The Bible has already given us that in the table and in baptism.

## The Regulative Principle and Conscience

Some churches that follow the normative principle make practices central to worship that we do not include — the use of images as worship aids, for instance, or foot-washing as a sacrament. Regarding images: the second commandment does not forbid the existence of images anywhere; it forbids worshiping God *through* images. (Even the tabernacle had cherubim and other images in it.) What is forbidden is using created things as helps or aids in worshiping God.

Regarding foot-washing: Jesus washed his disciples' feet in John 13, and then he explained the meaning himself. He was not instituting a new practice to be repeated in corporate worship. He was giving an object lesson: "I have just done the most menial thing imaginable. Go and serve one another in the same spirit." Sometimes that might look like washing someone's feet. Sometimes it might look like bringing someone a meal, or praying for them. It was an object lesson, not a prescription for perpetual observance.

Everything we do in our Sunday worship — the reading, the preaching, the praying, the singing, the sacraments — we do because Christ is building and regulating his church by his Word, and we are glad to be shaped by nothing less.
